entries.name: Racial Traits Overview
entries: The description of each race includes racial traits that are common to members of that race. The following entries appear among the traits of most races.

entries.name: Ability Score Increase
entries.description: Every race increases one or more of a character's ability scores.

entries.name: Age
entries.description: The age entry notes the age when a member of the race is considered an adult, as well as the race's expected lifespan. This information can help you decide how old your character is at the start of the game. You can choose any age for your character, which could provide an explanation for some of your ability scores. For example, if you play a young or very old character, your age could explain a particularly low Strength or Constitution score, while advanced age could account for a high Intelligence or Wisdom.

entries.name: Alignment Tendencies
entries: Most races have tendencies toward certain alignments, described in this entry. These are not binding for player characters, but considering why your dwarf is chaotic, for example, in defiance of lawful dwarf society can help you better define your character.

items: Lawful Good (LG): Creatures can be counted on to do the right thing as expected by society. Gold dragons, paladins, and most dwarves are lawful good.
Neutral Good (NG): Folk do the best they can to help others according to their needs. Many celestials, some cloud giants, and most gnomes are neutral good.
Chaotic Good (CG): Creatures act as their conscience directs, with little regard for what others expect. Copper dragons, many elves, and unicorns are chaotic good.
Lawful Neutral (LN): Individuals act in accordance with law, tradition, or personal codes. Many monks and some wizards are lawful neutral.
Neutral (N): Alignment of those who prefer to steer clear of moral questions and don't take sides, doing what seems best at the  time. Lizardfolk, most druids, and many humans are neutral.
Chaotic Neutral (CN): Creatures follow their whims, holding their personal freedom above all else. Many barbarians and rogues, and some bards, are chaotic neutral.
Lawful Evil (LE): Creatures methodically take what they want, within the limits of a code of tradition, loyalty, or order. Devils, blue dragons, and hobgoblins are lawful evil.
Neutral Evil (NE): Alignment of those who do whatever they can get away with, without compassion or qualms. Many drow, some cloud giants, and yugoloths are neutral evil.
Chaotic Evil (CE): Creatures act with arbitrary violence, spurred by their greed, hatred, or bloodlust. Demons, red dragons, and orcs are chaotic evil.

entries.name: Size Categories
entries: Characters of most races are Medium, a size category including creatures that are roughly 4 to 8 feet tall.
Members of a few races are Small (between 2 and 4 feet tall), which means that certain rules of the game affect them differently.
The most important of these rules is that Small characters have trouble wielding heavy weapons, as explained in chapter 6.

entries.name: Speed
entries.description: Your speed determines how far you can move when traveling (chapter 8) and fighting (chapter 9).

details.text: Every character and monster has a speed, which is the distance in feet that the character or monster can walk in 1 round. This number assumes short bursts of energetic movement in the midst of a life-threatening situation.

items: The following rules determine how far a character or monster can move in a minute, an hour, or a day.

entries.name: Languages
entries.content: By virtue of your race, your character can speak, read, and write certain languages. Chapter 4 lists the most common languages of the D&D multiverse. Your race indicates the languages your character can speak by default, and your background might give you access to one or more additional languages of your choice. Note these languages on your character sheet. Choose your languages from the Standard Languages table, or choose one that is common in your campaign. With your DM's permission, you can instead choose a language from the Exotic Languages table or a secret language, such as thieves' cant or the tongue of druids. Some of these languages are actually families of languages with many dialects. For example, the Primordial language includes the Auran, Aquan, Ignan, and Terran dialects, one for each of the four elemental planes. Creatures that speak different dialects of the same language can communicate with one another. Some backgrounds also allow characters to learn additional languages beyond those given by race. See 'Languages' earlier in this chapter.

entries.name: Subraces
entries: Members of a subrace have the traits of the parent race in addition to the traits specified for their subrace.
Relationships among subraces vary significantly from race to race and world to world.

entries.name: Dwarven Subraces
entries: In the Dragonlance campaign setting, mountain dwarves and hill dwarves live together as different clans of the same people, but in the Forgotten Realms, they live far apart in separate kingdoms and call themselves shield dwarves and gold dwarves, respectively.

entries.name: Dwarven Culture
entries: Dwarves are solid and enduring like the mountains they love, weathering the passage of centuries with stoic endurance and little change.
They revere the traditions of their clans, tracing their ancestry back to the founding of their most ancient strongholds in the youth of the world.
Part of those traditions is devotion to the gods of the dwarves, who uphold the dwarven ideals of industrious labor, skill in battle, and devotion to the forge.

lore.name: Dwarven Kingdoms
lore.text: Dwarven kingdoms stretch deep beneath the mountains where the dwarves mine gems and precious metals and forge items of wonder. They love the beauty and artistry of precious metals and fine jewelry, and in some dwarves this lust festers into avarice. Whatever wealth they can't find in their mountains, they gain through trade. They dislike boats, so enterprising humans and halflings frequently handle trade in dwarven goods along water routes. Trustworthy members of other races are welcome in dwarf settlements, though some areas are off-limits even to them.

lore.name: Social Structure
lore.text: The chief unit of dwarven society is the dan, and dwarves highly value social standing. Even dwarves who live far from their own kingdoms cherish their dan identities and affiliations, recognize related dwarves, and invoke their ancestors' names in oaths and curses. To be danless is the worst fate that can befall a dwarf.

lore.name: Roles Outside Kingdoms
lore.text: Dwarves in other lands are typically artisans, especially weaponsmiths, armorers, and jewelers. Some become mercenaries or bodyguards, highly sought after for their courage and loyalty.

entries.name: Dwarf Motivations
entries: Dwarves are often driven by a desire for treasure, whether for personal gain, a specific purpose, or altruistic reasons to help others.
Some dwarves are inspired by a deity's command or a direct calling to serve one of the dwarf gods, seeking to bring glory to their patron.
Clan and ancestry are significant motivators. A dwarf might seek to restore their clan's lost honor, avenge an ancient wrong, or earn a new place within their clan after exile.
Dwarves may also search for ancestral relics, such as a lost weapon wielded by a mighty ancestor.

entries.name: Interactions with Other Races
entries: Dwarves generally get along with most other races, though trust is difficult to earn due to their long lifespans compared to shorter-lived races like humans.
A dwarf's saying, 'The difference between an acquaintance and a friend is about a hundred years,' highlights the challenge of building trust with humans.

entries.name: Elves
entries: Elves are described as unreliable, with unpredictable behavior. They may sing or draw swords when excited, showing a mix of frivolity and combat readiness.
Despite their capriciousness, elves are valued for their craftsmanship and loyalty in battle, especially against goblins and ores.

entries.name: Half-Elves
entries: Half-elves are portrayed as pleasant but unreliable, with their ambitions often leading to grand projects that fail to materialize.
Their ability to build empires or create treasures is questioned, suggesting a lack of seriousness in their pursuits.

entries.name: Humans
entries: Humans are seen as fleeting companions, as their lives are short compared to dwarves. Trust is hard to earn, as a human may die before forming a bond.
However, humans can sometimes leave behind descendants (daughters or granddaughters) who inherit their qualities, making them potential allies.
Humans are characterized by their dedication to causes, whether it's a dragon's hoard or an empire's throne, though this often leads to trouble.

entries.name: Sleender and Graceful
entries: Elves appear hauntingly beautiful to humans and members of many other races. They are slightly shorter than humans on average, ranging from well under 5 feet tall to just over 6 feet. They are more slender than humans, weighing only 100 to 145 pounds. Males and females are about the same height, and males are only marginally heavier than females. Elves' coloration encompasses the normal human range and also includes skin in shades of copper, bronze, and almost bluish-white, hair of green and blue, and eyes like pools of liquid gold and silver. Elves have no facial or body hair. They favor elegant clothing in bright colors, and they enjoy simple yet lovely jewelry.

entries.name: Elf
traits: Long Lifespan: Elves can live well over 700 years, giving them a broad perspective on events that might trouble shorter-lived races more deeply.
Calm and Curious: Elves are more often amused than excited, and more likely to be curious than greedy. They tend to remain aloof and unfazed by petty happenstance.
Focused and Relentless: When pursuing a goal, whether adventuring on a mission or learning a new skill or art, elves can be focused and relentless.
Slow to Form Bonds: Elves are slow to make friends and enemies, and even slower to forget them. They reply to petty insults with disdain and to serious insults with vengeance.
Flexible in Danger: Like the branches of a young tree, elves are flexible in the face of danger. They trust in diplomacy and compromise to resolve differences before escalating to violence.
Strategic Retreat: Elves have been known to retreat from intrusions into their woodland homes, confident they can wait invaders out.
Martial Proficiency: When the need arises, elves reveal a stern martial side, demonstrating skill with sword, bow, and strategy.

entries.name: Hidden Woodland Realms
entries: Most elves dwell in small forest villages hidden among the trees. Elves hunt game, gather food, and grow vegetables, and their skill and magic allow them to support themselves without the need for clearing and plowing land. They are talented artisans, crafting finely worked clothes and art objects. Their contact with outsiders is usually limited, though a few elves make a good living by trading crafted items for metals (which they have no interest in mining).
Elves encountered outside their own lands are commonly traveling minstrels, artists, or sages. Human nobles compete for the services of elf instructors to teach swordplay or magic to their children.

entries.name: Elven Exploration and Adventuring Motivations
entries: Elves are driven by wanderlust and a desire for exploration and discovery due to their long lifespans, allowing them to experience centuries of adventure.
They dislike the rigid pace of human society, which is regulated daily but changes over decades, leading them to pursue careers that offer freedom and self-determined travel.
Elves often seek to exercise their martial prowess or gain greater magical power through adventuring.
Some elves join rebels fighting against oppression, while others become champions of moral causes.

entries.name: Name Conventions
entries: Elves are considered children until they declare themselves adults, some time after the hundredth birthday, and before this period they are called by child names.

On declaring adulthood, an elf selects an adult name, although those who knew them as a youngster might continue to use the child name. Each elf's adult name is a unique creation, though it might reflect the names of respected individuals or other family members.

Little distinction exists between male and female names; the groupings here reflect only general tendencies. Every elf bears a family name, typically a combination of other Elvish words. Some elves traveling among humans translate their family names into Common, but others retain the Elvish version.

entries.name: Halfling Lore
entries: Halflings seek comfort in home, desiring peace, quiet, and security from threats. They often live in agricultural communities or nomadic bands, valuing food, hearth, and companionship.
Despite their love for stability, some halflings are wanderers drawn to exploration, though they still cherish the idea of a home, whether a wagon, raft, or settled dwelling.

entries.name: Physical Description
entries: Skin ranges from tan to pale with a ruddy cast
Hair is usually brown or sandy brown and wavy
Eyes are brown or hazel
Men often sport long sideburns; beards and mustaches are rare
Wear simple, comfortable, and practical clothes, favoring bright colors

entries.name: Cultural Traits
entries: Survive by avoiding notice or offending larger creatures
Prefer practicality over ostentation; wealthiest keep treasures locked in cellars
Seek straightforward solutions to problems and avoid dithering
Stand about 3 feet tall and weigh between 40 and 45 pounds

entries.name: Kino Ano Curious
entries: Halflings are an affable and cheerful people. They cherish the bonds of family and friendship as well as the comforts of hearth and home, harboring few dreams of gold or glory. Even adventurers among them usually venture into the world for reasons of community, friendship, wanderlust, and curiosity. They love discovering new things, even simple things, such as an exotic food or an unfamiliar style of clothing.
Halflings are easily moved to pity and hate to see any living thing suffer. They are generous, happily sharing what they have even in lean times.

entries.name: Blend into the Crowd
entries: Halflings are adept at fitting into a community of humans, dwarves, and elves, making themselves valuable and welcome. The combination of their inherent stealth and their unassuming nature helps halflings to avoid unwanted attention.

Halflings work readily with others, and they are loyal to their friends, whether halfling or otherwise. They can display remarkable ferocity when their friends, families, or communities are threatened.

entries.name: Lore
entries: Most halflings live in small, peaceful communities with large farms and well-kept graves. They rarely build kingdoms of their own or even hold much land beyond their quiet shires. They typically don't recognize any sort of halfling nobility or royalty, instead looking to family elders to guide them. Families preserve their traditional ways despite the rise and fall of empires.

Many halflings live among other races, where the halflings' hard work and loyal outlook offer them abundant rewards and creature comforts. Some halfling communities travel as a way of life, driving wagons or guiding boats from place to place and maintaining no permanent home.

entries.name: Lore
entries: Elves. 'They're 50 beautiful! Their faces, their music, their grace and all. It's like they stepped out of a wonderful dream. But there's no telling what's going on behind their smiling faces—surely more than they ever let on.'

Humans. 'Humans are a 101 like us, really. At least some of them are. Step out of the castles and keeps, go talk to the farmers and herders, and you'll find good, solid folk. Not that there's anything wrong with the barons and soldiers—you have to admire their conviction. And by protecting their own lands, they protect us as well.'

entries.text: Halflings usually set out on the adventurer's path to defend their communities, support their friends, or explore a wide and wonder-filled world. For them, adventuring is less a career than an opportunity or sometimes a necessity.

entries.name: Name Structure
entries: A halfling has a given name, a family name, and possibly a nickname. Family names are often nicknames that stuck so tenaciously they have been passed down through the generations.

entries.name: A Broad Spectrum
traits: Physically diverse with height ranging from 5 feet to over 6 feet tall, and weight from 125 to 250 pounds.
Skin shades range from nearly black to very pale, with hair colors spanning black to blond (curly, kinky, or straight).
Males may have facial hair ranging from sparse to thick.
Many individuals have nonhuman bloodline hints (elf, ore, or other lineages).

entries.name: Variety in All Things
entries: Humans are the most adaptable and ambitious people among the common races. They have widely varying tastes, morals, and customs in the many different lands where they have settled. When they settle, though, they stay: they build cities to last for the ages, and great kingdoms that can persist for long centuries. An individual human might have a relatively short life span, but a human nation or culture preserves traditions with origins far beyond the reach of any single human's memory. They live fully in the present, making them well suited to the adventuring life—but also plan for the future, striving to leave a lasting legacy. Individually and as a group, humans are adaptable opportunists, and they stay alert to changing political and social dynamics.
Elves are proud and independent, often viewing humans as intruders. If an elf can get past that damned racial pride and actually treat you like an equal, you can learn a lot from them.
Halflings are known for their love of good food and stories, often gathering around a warm fire. If halflings had a shred of ambition, they might really amount to something.

entries.name: Human Traits
entries: Humans establish enduring institutions such as temples, governments, libraries, and legal codes to preserve traditions, contrasting with elf clans, dwarf clans, and halfling elders who pass down lore through generations.
Humans pursue immortality through legacy rather than literal immortality, ensuring their impact endures after death.
While some humans exhibit xenophobia, their societies are generally inclusive, welcoming nonhumans in larger numbers than nonhuman lands accommodate humans.

entries.name: Overview
entries: Humans who seek adventure are the most daring and ambitious members of a daring and ambitious race. They seek to earn glory in the eyes of their fellows by amassing power, wealth, and fame. More than other people, humans champion causes rather than territories or groups.

entries.name: Name Variations
entries: Humans have no typical names; parents may draw from other languages (e.g., Dwarvish, Elvish) or regional/cultural traditions.
Names often reflect ancestral naming traditions or regional cultural influences.

entries.name: Cultural and Physical Diversity
entries: Material culture, architecture, cuisine, music, and literature vary drastically by region (e.g., Silver Marches vs. Turmish or Impiltur).
Physical characteristics reflect ancient human migrations, resulting in diverse coloration and features.

entries.name: Ethnic Groups
entries: Nine widely recognized ethnic groups in the Forgotten Realms, with over a dozen localized variants in Faerun.
Examples of distinct regions: Silver Marches, Turmish, Impiltur, and Kara-Tur.

entries.name: Physical Description
entries: Shorler and slighler in build than mos I olher humans, Calishites have dusky brown skin, hair, and eyes. They're found primarily in soulhwest Faerun.

entries.name: Names
items: Male: Aseir, Bardeid, Haseid, Khemed, Mehmen, Sudeiman, Zasheir
Female: Alala, Ceidil, Hama, Jasmal, Meilil, Seipora, Yasheira, Zasheida
Surnames: Basha, Dumein, Jassan, Khalid, Moslana, Pashar, Rein

traits.name: Names
items: Male: Darvin, Dorn, Evendur, Gorstag, Grim, Helm, Malark, Morn, Randal, Stedd
Female: Arveene, Esvele, Jhessail, Kerri, Lureene, Miri, Rowan, Shandri, Tessele
Surnames: Amblecrown, Buckman, Dundragon, Evenwood, Greycastle, Tallstag

trait.name: Dan Names First
entries: Dragonborn have personal names given at birth, but they put their dan names first as a mark of honor. A childhood name or nickname is often used among companions as a descriptive term or a term of endearment. The name might recall an event or center on a habit.

action.name: Names
entries: Male Names: Arjhan. Balasar. Bharash, Donaar, Ghesh. Heskan, Kriv. Medrash. Mehen. Nadarr. Pandjed, Patrin, Rhogar, Shamash. Shedinn. Tarhun. Torinn
Female Names: Akra. Biri. Daar. Farideh, Harann. HaviJar, Jheri, Kava, Korinn. Mishann, NaJa, Perra, Raiann. Sora. Surina. Thava, Uadjit
Childhood Names: Climber. Earbender. Leaper. Pious. Shieldbiter. Zealous

Clan Names: Clethtinthiallor. Daardendrian. Delm. Drachedandion. Fenkenkabradon. Kepeshkmolik. Kerrhylon. Kimbatuul. Linxakasendalor. Myastan. Nemmonis. Norixius. Ophinshtalajiir. Prexijandilin. Shestendeliath. Turnuroth. Verthisathurgiesh. Yarjerit

trait.name: Draconic Ancestry
entries: Choose one type of dragon from the Draconic Ancestry table. Your breath weapon and damage resistance are determined by the dragon type, as shown in the table.

action.name: Breath Weapon
entries: {@atk action} Use your action to exhale destructive energy. Your draconic ancestry determines the size, shape, and damage type of the exhalation. Each creature in the area must make a {@save} save (type determined by your draconic ancestry). On a failed save, a creature takes {@damage 2d6} damage, half as much on a successful save. The damage increases to 3d6 at 6th level, 4d6 at 11th level, and 5d6 at 16th level. After using your breath weapon, you cannot use it again until you complete a short or long rest.

entries.name: Physical Description
entries: A gnome's energy and enthusiasm for living shines through every inch of his or her tiny body. Gnomes average slightly over 3 feet tall and weigh 40 to 45 pounds. Their tan or brown faces are usually adorned with broad smiles (beneath their prodigious noses), and their bright eyes shine with excitement. Their fair hair has a tendency to stick out in every direction, as if expressing the gnome's insatiable interest in everything around.

entries.name: Personality
entries: A gnome's persona!ity is writ large in his or ber appearance. A male gnome's beard, in contrast to his wild hair, is kept carefully trimmed but often styled into curious forks or neat points. A gnome's clothing, though usually made in modest earth tones, is elaborately decorated with embroidery, embossing, or gleaming jewels.

entries.name: Delighted Dedication
entries: Gnomes are Small creatures with a +2 bonus to Intelligence, and their speed is 30 feet.
They speak Common and choose one additional language from the list of languages known to the GM.
Gnomes are known for their love of jokes, puns, and pranks, yet they are also dedicated to serious tasks such as engineering, alchemy, tinkering, and invention.
They embrace bold risks and dreaming large, often making mistakes and laughing at themselves in the process of perfecting their craft.

entries.name: Languages
entries: Common (proficient)
One additional language from the list of languages known to the GM

entries.name: Traits
entries: Love for jokes, puns, and pranks
Dedication to serious tasks (engineering, alchemy, tinkering, invention)
Bold risks and dreaming large
Will to make mistakes and laugh at oneself

entries.name: Half-Ore
entries.description: Half-ores' grayish pigmentation, sloping foreheads, jutting jaws, prominent leelh, and lowering builds make their orcish heritage plain for all to see. Half-ores stand between 6 and 7 feet tall and usually weigh between 180 and 250 pounds.
lore: Ores regard battle scars as tokens of pride and ornamental scars as things of beauty. Other scars, though, mark an ore or half-ore as a former slave or a disgraced exile. Any half-ore who has lived among or near ores has scars, whether they are marks of humiliation or of pride, recounting their past exploits and injuries. Such a half-ore living among humans might display these scars proudly or hide them in shame.

traits.name: Mark of Gruumsh
entries: Half-orcs are subject to the influence of the one-eyed god Gruumsh. While their human blood moderates the impact of their orcish heritage, they still feel the whispers of Gruumsh in their dreams, calling them to unleash the rage that simmers within them.
Some half-orcs embrace Gruumsh's influence, feeling exultation alongside his wrath. Others shudder with fear and loathing, resisting his call.

traits.name: Emotional Intensity
entries: Rage doesn't merely quicken their pulse—it makes their bodies burn. An insult stings like acid, and sadness saps their strength. Yet they laugh loudly and heartily, finding joy in simple pleasures like feasting, drinking, wrestling, drumming, and wild dancing.
Half-orcs are short-tempered and prone to sullenness, favoring action over contemplation and fighting over argument.

traits.name: Self-Control
entries: The most accomplished half-orcs possess enough self-control to navigate civilized lands, balancing their primal instincts with discipline.

lore.name: Gruumsh's Influence
lore.text: The one-eyed god Gruumsh exerts a profound influence over half-orcs, shaping their emotions and actions. While not inherently evil, the latent rage and chaos of their orcish heritage can manifest in unpredictable ways.

entries.name: Societal Context
entries: Tieflings subsist in small minorities found mostly in human cities or towns, often in the roughest quarters of those places, where they grow up to be swindlers.
Other minority populations exist in enclaves where they are treated with more respect.

entries.name: Personality Traits
entries: Lacking a homeland, tieflings know they must make their own way in the world and be strong to survive.
They are not quick to trust anyone who claims to be a friend. However, when a tiefl,ing's companions demonstrate trust, they learn to extend that trust in return.
Once a tiefling gives someone loyalty, they become a firm friend or ally for life.

entries.name: Barbarian
entries: A fierce warrior of primitive background who can enter a battle rage

items: Light and medium armor, shields, simple and martial weapons

entries.name: Bard
entries: An inspiring magician whose power echoes the music of creation

items: Light armor, simple weapons, hand crossbows, longswords, rapiers, shortswords

entries.name: Cleric
entries: A priestly champion who wields divine magic in service of a higher power

items: Light and medium armor, shields, simple weapons

entries.name: Druid
entries: A priest of the Old Faith, wielding the powers of nature—moonlight and plant growth, fire and lightning—and adopting animal forms

items: Light and medium armor (nonmetal), shields (nonmetal), clubs, daggers, darts, javelins, maces, quarterstaves, scimitars, sickles, slings, spears

entries.name: Fighter
entries: A master of martial combat, skilled with a variety of weapons and armor

items: All armor, shields, simple and martial weapons

entries.name: Monk
entries: A master of martial arts, harnessing the power of the body in pursuit of physical and spiritual perfection

entries.name: Paladin
entries: A holy warrior bound to a sacred oath

items: All armor, shields, simple and martial weapons

entries.name: Ranger
entries: A warrior who uses martial prowess and nature magic to combat threats on the edges of civilization

items: Light and medium armor, shields, simple and martial weapons

entries.name: Rogue
entries: A scoundrel who uses stealth and trickery to overcome obstacles and enemies

items: Light armor, simple weapons, hand crossbows, longswords, rapiers, shortswords

entries.name: Sorcerer
entries: A spellcaster who draws on inherent magic from a gift or bloodline

items: Daggers, darts, slings, quarterstaffs, light crossbows

entries.name: Warlock
entries: A wielder of magic that is derived from a bargain with an extraplanar entity

entries.name: Wizard
entries: A scholarly magic-user capable of manipulating the structures of reality

items: Daggers, darts, slings, quarterstaffs, light crossbows

entries.name: Creating a Barbarian
entries: When creating a barbarian character, think about where your character comes from and his or her place in the world. Talk with your DM about an appropriate origin for your barbarian. Did you come from a distant land, making you a stranger in the area of the campaign? Or is the campaign set in a rough-and-tumble frontier where barbarians are common?

What led you to take up the adventuring life? Were you lured to settled lands by the promise of riches? Did you join forces with soldiers of those lands to face a shared threat? Did monsters or an invading horde drive you out of your homeland, making you a rootless refugee? Perhaps you were a prisoner of war, brought in chains to "civilized" lands and only now able to win your freedom. Or you might have been cast out from your people because of a crime you committed, a taboo you violated, or a coup that removed you from a position of authority.

entries.name: Quick Build
entries.description:
Guidelines for creating characters of various classes quickly:

- **Barbarian**: Strength (highest), Constitution; choose Outlander background.
- **Bard**: Charisma (highest), Dexterity; choose Entertainer background; select Dancing Lights and Vicious Mockery cantrips, plus 1st-level spells: Charm Person, Detect Magic, Healing Word, Thunderwave.
- **Cleric**: Wisdom (highest), Strength/Constitution; choose Acolyte background.
- **Druid**: Wisdom (highest), Constitution; choose Hermit background.
- **Fighter**: Strength/Dexterity (highest, based on melee/archery focus); Constitution/Intelligence (if adopting Eldritch Knight); choose Soldier background.
- **Monk**: Dexterity (highest), Wisdom; choose Hermit background.
- **Paladin**: Strength (highest), Charisma; choose Noble background.
- **Ranger**: Dexterity (highest), Wisdom; choose Outlander background (Strength > Dexterity for two-weapon fighters).
- **Rogue**: Dexterity (highest); Intelligence (for Investigation or Arcane Trickster) or Charisma (for deception); choose Charlatan background.
- **Sorcerer**: Charisma (highest), Constitution; choose Hermit background; select Light, Prestidigitation, Ray of Frost, Shocking Grasp cantrips; plus 1st-level spells.
- **Wizard**: Intelligence (highest), Dexterity; choose Scholar background; select Illusionary Magic, Magic Missile, and other cantrips; plus 1st-level spells.

entries.name: Multiclassing Considerations
entries.text: When you gain a new level in a class, you get its features for that level. A few features, however, have additional rules when you're multiclassing: Channel Divinity, Extra Attack, Unarmored Defense, and Spellcasting.

entries.name: Hit Points
entries: Hit Dice: 1d12 per barbarian level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 12 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d12 (or 7) + your Constitution modifier per barbarian level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d8 per cleric level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 8 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d8 (or 5) + your Constitution modifier per cleric level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d8 per druid level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 8 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d8 (or 5) + your Constitution modifier per druid level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d10 per fighter level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 10 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d10 (or 6) + your Constitution modifier per fighter level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d8 per monk level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 8 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d8 (or 5) + your Constitution modifier per monk level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d10 per paladin level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 10 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d10 (or 6) + your Constitution modifier per paladin level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d10 per ranger level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 10 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d10 (or 6) + your Constitution modifier per ranger level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d6 per rogue level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 6 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d6 (or 4) + your Constitution modifier per rogue level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d6 per sorcerer level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 6 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d6 (or 4) + your Constitution modifier per sorcerer level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d8 per warlock level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 8 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d8 (or 5) + your Constitution modifier per warlock level after 1st.
Hit Dice: 1d6 per wizard level. Hit Points at 1st Level: 6 + your Constitution modifier. Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d6 (or 4) + your Constitution modifier per wizard level after 1st.

entries.name: Constitution Modifier and Hit Points
entries: Your Constitution modifier contributes to your hit points. Typically, you add your Constitution modifier to each Hit Die you roll for your hit points.

If your Constitution modifier changes, your hit point maximum changes as well, as though you had the new modifier from 1st level. For example, if you raise your Constitution score when you reach 4th level and your Constitution modifier increases from +1 to +2, you adjust your hit point maximum as though the modifier had always been +2. So you add 3 hit points for your first three levels, and then roll your hit points for 4th level using your new modifier. Or if you're 7th level and some effect lowers your Constitution score so as to reduce your Constitution modifier by 1, your hit point maximum is reduced by 7.

entries.name: Hit Points as a Metric
entries: Hit points represent a combination of physical and mental durability, the will to live, and luck. Creatures with more hit points are more difficult to kill. Those with fewer hit points are more fragile.

A creature's current hit points (usually just called hit points) can be any number from the creature's hit point maximum down to 0. This number changes frequently as a creature takes damage or receives healing.
Whenever a creature takes damage, that damage is subtracted from its hit points. The loss of hit points has no effect on a creature's capabilities until the creature drops to 0 hit points.

items: Each background gives a character proticiency in two skills.
Most backgrounds give a character proticiency with one or more tools.
If a character would gain the same proticiency from two different sources, he or she can choose a different proticiency of the same kind (skill or tool) instead.
When you gain a level in a class other than your first, you gain only some of that class's starting proficiencies, as shown in the Multiclassing Proficiencies table.
a martial weapon and a shield

a shortbow and quiver of 20 arrows

entries.name: Starting Equipment
entries.content: You start with the following equipment, in addition to the equipment granted by your background. Each background provides a package of starting equipment. If you use the optional rule from chapter 5 to spend coin on gear, you do not receive the starting equipment from your background.

entries.name: Equipment Selection
entries.content: Choose one of the following options for your starting equipment, based on your class and background. For example, a Fighter might choose a greataxe, while a Druid might select a longbow and quiver of arrows.

entries.name: Equipment Overrides
entries.content: If you use the optional rule from chapter 5 to spend coin on gear, you do not receive the starting equipment from your background. Instead, you can allocate gold pieces to purchase equipment as described in the rule.

entries.name: Equipment Variants
entries.content: Some equipment options provide multiple choices (e.g., a greataxe or any martial melee weapon). Select one option from each set to determine your starting equipment.

entries.name: Rage
entries: You can enter a rage as a bonus action on your turn. While raging, you gain the following benefits if you aren't wearing heavy armor:

items: You have advantage on Strength checks and Strength saving throws.
When you make a melee weapon attack using Strength, you gain a bonus to the damage roll that increases as you gain levels as a barbarian, as shown in the Rage Damage column of the Barbarian table.
You have resistance to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage.

entries.name: Spellcasting Restriction
entries: If you are able to cast spells, you can't cast them or concentrate on them while raging.

entries.name: Duration and End Conditions
entries: Your rage lasts for 1 minute. It ends early if you are knocked unconscious or if your turn ends and you haven't attacked a hostile creature since your last turn or taken damage since then. You can also end your rage on your turn as a bonus action.

entries.name: Rage Limit
entries: Once you have raged the number of times shown for your barbarian level in the Rages column of the Barbarian table, you must finish a long rest before you can rage again.

entries.name: Unarmored Defense
entries: While you are not wearing any armor, your Armor Class equals 10 + your Dexterity modifier + your Constitution modifier. You can use a shield and still gain this benefit.
Beginning at 1st level, while you are wearing no armor and not wielding a shield, your AC equals 10 + your Dexterity modifier + your Wisdom modifier.
If you already have the Unarmored Defense feature, you can't gain it again from another class.

entries.content: Starting at 2nd level, you can throw aside all concern for defense to attack with fierce desperation. When you make your first attack on your turn, you can decide to attack recklessly. Doing so gives you advantage on melee weapon attack rolls using Strength during this turn, but attack rolls against you have advantage until your next turn.

entries.text: At 2nd level, you gain an uncanny sense of when things nearby aren't as they should be, giving you an edge when you dodge away from danger.
You have advantage on Dexterity saving throws against effects that you can see, such as traps and spells. To gain this benefit, you can't be blinded, deafened, or incapacitated.
This feature is part of the Rogue class description in the Player's Handbook.

entries.name: Primal Path
entries: At 3rd level, you choose a path that shapes the nature of your rage. Choose the Path of the Berserker or the Path of the Totem Warrior, both detailed at the end of the class description.
Your choice grants you features at 3rd level and again at 6th, 10th, and 14th levels.

entries.name: Ability Score Improvement
entries.description: When you reach 4th level, and again at 8th, 12th, 16th, and 19th level, you can increase one ability score of your choice by 2, or you can increase two ability scores of your choice by 1. As normal, you can't increase an ability score above 20 using this feature.

entries.name: Extra Attack
entries.description: Beginning at 5th level, you can attack twice, instead of once, whenever you take the Attack action on your turn. The number of attacks increases to three when you reach 11th level in this class and to four when you reach 20th level in this class.

entries.name: Combining with Other Features
entries: If you gain the Extra Attack class feature from more than one class, the features don't add together. You can't make more than two attacks with this feature unless it says you do (as the fighter's version of Extra Attack does).
Similarly, the warlock's eldritch invocation {@item Thirsting Blade|PHB.Rule.Thirsting Blade} doesn't give you additional attacks if you also have Extra Attack.

entries.name: Description
entries: Starting at 5th level, your speed increases by 10 feet while you aren't wearing heavy armor.

entries.name: Feral Instinct
entries: By 7th level, your instincts are so honed that you have advantage on initiative rolls.

Additionally, if you are surprised at the beginning of combat and aren't incapacitated, you can act normally on your first turn, but only if you enter your rage before doing anything else on that turn.

items: Beginning at 9th level, you can roll one additional weapon damage die when determining the extra damage for a critical hit with a melee attack.
This increases to two additional dice at 13th level and three additional dice at 17th level.

entries.name: Relentless Rage
entries.description: When you drop to 0 hit points while raging and don't die outright, you can make a {@dc 10} Constitution saving throw. On a success, you drop to 1 hit point instead. Each subsequent use of this feature increases the DC by 5. After a short or long rest, the DC resets to 10.

entries.name: Persistent Rage
entries.description: Your rage is so fierce that it ends early only if you fall unconscious or if you choose to end it.

entries.name: Indomitable Might
entries.description: Beginning at 18th level, if your total for a Strength check is less than your Strength score, you can use that score in place of the total.

entries.name: 20th Level Feature
entries: At 20th level, you embody the power of the wilds. Your Strength and Constitution scores increase by 4. Your maximum for those scores is now 24.

entries.name: Primal Paths
entries: Rage burns in every barbarian's heart. A furnace that drives him or her toward greatness. Different barbarians attribute their rage to different sources, however. For some, it is an internal reservoir where pain, grief, and anger are forged into a fury hard as steel. Others see it as a spiritual blessing, a gift of a totem animal.

entries.name: Path of the Berserker
entries: For some barbarians, rage is a means to an end—that end being violence. The Path of the Berserker is a path of untrammeled fury, slick with blood. As you enter the berserker's rage, you thrill in the chaos of battle, heedless of your own health or well-being.

entries.name: Frenzy
entries.description: Starting at 3rd level, you can go into a frenzy when you rage. For the duration of your rage, you can make a single melee weapon attack as a bonus action on each of your turns after the first. When your rage ends, you suffer one level of exhaustion (as described in appendix A).

entries.name: Mindless Rage
entries.description: Beginning at 6th level, you can't be charmed or frightened while raging. If you are charmed or frightened when you enter your rage, the effect is suspended for the duration of the rage.

items: Target: One creature within 30 feet of you that can see or hear you
Saving Throw: Wisdom (DC 8 + proficiency bonus + Charisma modifier)
Effect: Frightened until the end of its next turn
Extension: On subsequent turns, use action to extend duration until end of its next turn
Duration End: If the creature ends its turn out of line of sight or more than 60 feet away
Cooldown: Cannot use this feature on the creature again for 24 hours if it succeeds on the saving throw

entries.name: Retaliation
entries.description: When you take damage from a creature within 5 feet of you, you can use your reaction to make a melee weapon attack against that creature.

entries.name: Path of the Totem Warrior
entries: The Path of the Totem Warrior is a spiritual journey where a barbarian accepts a spirit animal as guide, protector, and inspiration. In battle, your totem spirit fills you with supernatural might, adding magical fuel to your barbarian rage.
Most barbarian tribes consider a totem animal to be kin to a particular elan. It is unusual for an individual to have more than one totem animal spirit, though exceptions exist.

entries.name: Spirit Seeker
entries: A path that seeks attunement with the natural world, granting kinship with beasts.
At 3rd level, you can cast the {@spell Beast Sense|PHB} and {@spell Speak with Animals|PHB} spells as rituals, as described in Chapter 10.

entries.name: Totem Spirit
entries.description: When you adopt this path, you choose a totem spirit and gain its feature. You must make or acquire a physical totem object—an amulet or similar adornment—that incorporates fur or feathers, claws, teeth, or bones of the totem animal. As your option, you also gain minor physical attributes that are reminiscent of your totem spirit. For example, if you have a bear totem spirit, you might be unusually hairy and thick-skinned, or if your totem is the eagle, your eyes turn bright yellow.

features.name: Bear
features.description: While raging, you have resistance to all damage except psychic damage. The spirit of the bear makes you tough enough to stand up to any punishment.

features.name: Eagle
features.description: While you're raging and aren't wearing heavy armor, other creatures have disadvantage on opportunity attack rolls against you. And you can use the Dash action as a bonus action on your turn. The spirit of the eagle makes you into a predator who can weave through the fray with ease.

features.name: Wolf
features.description: While you're raging, your friends have advantage on melee attack rolls against any creature within 5 feet of you that is hostile to you. The spirit of the wolf makes you a leader of hunters.

notes.content: Your totem animal might be an animal related to those listed here but more appropriate to your homeland. For example, you could choose a hawk or vulture in place of an eagle.

entries.name: Aspect of the Beast
entries: At 6th level, you gain a magical benefit based on the totem animal of your choice. You can choose the same animal you selected at 3rd level or a different one.

entries.name: Bear
entries: You gain the might of a bear. Your carrying capacity (including maximum load and maximum lift) is doubled, and you have advantage on Strength checks made to push, pull, lift, or break objects.

entries.name: Eagle
entries: You gain the eyesight of an eagle. You can see up to 1 mile away with no difficulty. You can discern even fine details as though looking at something no more than 100 feet away from you. Additionally, dim light doesn't impose disadvantage on your Wisdom (Perception) checks.

entries.name: Wolf
entries: You gain the hunting sensibilities of a wolf. You can track other creatures while traveling at a fast pace, and you can move stealthily while traveling at a normal pace (see Chapter 8 for rules on travel pace).

entries.name: Spirit Walker 10th-Level Feature
entries: At 10th level, you can cast the {@spell Commune with Nature|PHB} spell, but only as a ritual.
When you do so, a spiritual version of one of the animals you chose for {@feature Totem Spirit|PHB} or {@feature Aspect of the Beast|PHB} appears to you to convey the information you seek.

entries.name: Totemic Attunement
entries: At 1st level, you gain a magical benefit based on a totem animal of your choice. You can choose the same animal you selected previously or a different one.

entries.name: Totem Animals
items: Bear: While you're raging, any creature within 5 feet of you that's hostile to you has disadvantage on ability checks against targets other than you or another character with this feature. An enemy is immune to this effect if it can't see or hear you or if it can't be frightened.

Eagle: While raging, you have a flying speed equal to your current walking speed. This benefit works only in short bursts; you fall if you end your turn in the air and nothing else is holding you aloft.
Wolf: While you're raging, you can use a bonus action on your turn to knock a Large or smaller creature prone when you hit it with a melee weapon attack.

entries.name: Bard Overview
entries: A bard weaves magic through words and music to inspire allies, demoralize foes, manipulate minds, create illusions, and even heal wounds.
Bards can take on roles as scholars, skalds, or scoundrels, using their artistry to influence others.

entries.name: Bard
entries.description: Bards are versatile magic users who harness echoes of the Words of Creation through song, speech, and spellcraft. They specialize in charms, illusions, and supportive magic, excelling in both magical combat and non-combat versatility. Bards can adapt to various roles, using their magic to inspire allies, hinder foes, and bolster defenses in melee when needed. Their knowledge spans diverse subjects, and they master talents through dedicated practice.
entries.lore: The multiverse was spoken into existence by the words of the gods, and bards seek to harness the lingering echoes of these primordial Words of Creation. This philosophy underpins their magic, which aims to shape reality through music and language.

entries.name: Learning from Experience
entries: True bards are rare, requiring hard study and natural talent beyond typical performers. They wander the world gathering lore, telling stories, and relying on audience gratitude. Their adventuring careers align with their desire to explore, learn, and discover, viewing every adventure as an opportunity to grow. Bards often accompany heroes to witness deeds firsthand, and many adopt heroic roles inspired by their tales.

entries.name: Creating a Bard
entries: Bards thrive on stories, whether those stories are true or not. Your character's background and motivations are not as important as the stories that he or she tells about them. Perhaps you had a secure and mundane childhood. There's no good story to be told about that, so you might paint yourself as an orphan raised by a hag in a dismal swamp. Or your childhood might be worthy of a story. Some bards acquire their magical music through extraordinary means, including the inspiration of fey or other supernatural creatures.

Did you serve an apprenticeship, studying under a master, following the more experienced bard until you were ready to strike out on your own? Or did you attend a college where you studied bardic lore and practiced your musical magic? Perhaps you were a young runaway or orphan, befriended by a wandering bard who became your mentor. Or you might have been a spoiled noble child tutored by a master. Perhaps you stumbled into the clutches of a hag, making a bargain for a musical gift in addition to your life and freedom, but at what cost?

entries.name: Hit Dice
entries: Hit Dice: 1d8 per bard level
Hit Points at 1st Level: 8 + your Constitution modifier
Hit Points at Higher Levels: 1d8 (or 5) + your Constitution modifier per bard level after 1st

entries.name: Spellcasting Rules
entries: Spell Slots: The number of spell slots is determined by class level and spellcasting feature. For example, Eldritch Knight uses the Eldritch Knight Spellcasting table, while Arcane Trickster uses the Arcane Trickster Spellcasting table. Spell slots are regained after a long rest.
Spell Save DC: Calculated as 8 + proficiency bonus + Intelligence modifier. Used for wizard spells.
Spell Attack Modifier: Proficiency bonus + Intelligence modifier. Used for spell attacks.
Cantrips: Wizards learn cantrips at 1st level (2 cantrips), with an additional cantrip at 10th level. Arcane Trickster learns 3 cantrips (mage hand and 2 others) with an additional at 10th level.

Multiclassing: Spellcasting capacity depends on combined levels in spellcasting classes. Use the Multiclass Spellcaster table to determine spell slots by summing levels in bard, cleric, druid, sorcerer, wizard, and half levels in paladin/ranger (rounded down), and a third of fighter/rogue levels (rounded down) for Eldritch Knight/Arcane Trickster.

Spells Known and Prepared: Each class determines spells independently. For example, a ranger 4/wizard 3 knows 3 1st-level ranger spells and 3 wizard cantrips. Spellcasting ability is based on the class associated with the spell.
Spellcasting Focus: Can be used only for spells from the class associated with that focus (e.g., holy symbol for cleric spells).

entries.name: Spellcasting with Multiple Classes
entries: If you have more than one spellcasting class, this table might give you spell slots of a level that is higher than the spells you know or can prepare. You can use those slots, but only to cast your lower-level spells.

If a lower-level spell that you cast, like burning bands, has an enhanced effect when cast using a higher-level slot, you can use the enhanced effect, even though you don't have any spells of that higher level.

Example: If you are the aforementioned ranger 4/wizard 3, you count as a 5th-level character when determining your spell slots: you have four 1st-level slots, three 2nd-level slots, and two 3rd-level slots. However, you don't know any 3rd-level spells, nor do you know any 2nd-level ranger spells. You can use the spell slots of those levels to cast the spells you do know—and potentially enhance their effects.

entries.name: Pact Magic Interaction
entries: If you have both the Spellcasting class feature and the Pact Magic class feature from the warlock class, you can use the spell slots you gain from the Pact Magic feature to cast spells you know or have prepared from classes with the Spellcasting class feature, and you can use the spell slots you gain from the Spellcasting class feature to cast warlock spells you know.

entries.name: Bard
entries: Know two cantrips of your choice from the bard spelllist.
Learn additional bard cantrips of your choice at higher levels as shown in the Cantrips Known column of the Bard table.

entries.name: Cleric
entries: Know three cantrips of your choice from the cleric spelllist.
Learn additional cleric cantrips of your choice at higher levels as shown in the Cantrips Known column of the Cleric table.

entries.name: Druid
entries: Know two cantrips of your choice from the druid spelllist.
Learn additional druid cantrips of your choice at higher levels as shown in the Cantrips Known column of the Druid table.

entries.name: Sorcerer
entries: Know four cantrips of your choice from the sorcerer spelllist.
Learn additional sorcerer cantrips of your choice at higher levels as shown in the Cantrips Known column of the Sorcerer table.

entries.name: Warlock
entries: Know two cantrips of your choice from the warlock spelllist.
Learn additional warlock cantrips of your choice at higher levels as shown in the Cantrips Known column of the Warlock table.

entries.name: Wizard
entries: Know three cantrips of your choice from the wizard spelllist.
Learn additional wizard cantrips of your choice at higher levels as shown in the Cantrips Known column of the Wizard table.

entries.name: Cantrip Mechanics
entries: A cantrip is a spell that can be cast at will, without using a spell slot and without being prepared in advance.
Repeated practice fixes the spell in the caster's mind, allowing them to cast it repeatedly.
A cantrip's spell level is 0.

entries.name: Spell Slot Mechanics
entries: Spell slots are used to cast spells of 1st level and higher. A spell slot of the spell's level or higher must be expended to cast a spell.
All expended spell slots are regained after finishing a long rest (see chapter 8 for resting rules).

entries.name: Class-Specific Tables
entries: Bard, Ranger, and Sorcerer classes have tables showing their available spell slots for 1st-level and higher spells.
Warlock spell slots are all of the same level, determined by the Warlock table. Higher-level spells require expending a slot of that level or higher.

items: Example: A Bard with 1st- and 2nd-level slots can cast 1st-level spells using either slot.
Example: A Warlock at 5th level has two 3rd-level slots and can cast 1st-level spells using those slots.

entries.name: Spells Known and Level Limits
entries: Warlock spell slots are limited to the level shown in the Warlock table. A spell must be of a level no higher than the slot level available.
At 6th level, a Warlock learns a new spell (1st, 2nd, or 3rd level). When gaining a level, you can replace a known spell with another from the Warlock spell list.

entries.name: Slot Expenditure and Regain
entries: Casting a spell consumes a slot of its level or higher. For example, a 1st-level spell fits any slot level, but a 9th-level spell requires a 9th-level slot.
Finishing a long rest restores all expended spell slots.

entries.name: Exceptions
entries: Some characters and monsters can cast spells without using spell slots (e.g., monks of the Way of the Four Elements, warlocks with certain eldritch invocations, and pit fiends).

entries.name: Cross-References
entries: See {@rule PHB.Class.Warlock} for Warlock-specific spell slot rules.
See {@rule PHB.Rule.Rest} for long rest mechanics.

entities.name: Spells Known of 1st Level and Higher

entries.name: Spells Known
entries: At 1st level, you know four 1st-level spells of your choice from the bard spelllist.
When you reach 3rd level in this class, you can learn one new spell of 1st or 2nd level.
When you gain a level in this class, you can choose one of the bard spells you know and replace it with another spell from the bard spelllist, which must be of a level for which you have spell slots.

entries.name: Spellcasting Ability
entries: Charisma is your spellcasting ability for bard spells. Your magic comes from the heart and soul you pour into the performance of your music or oration. You use your Charisma whenever a spell refers to your spellcasting ability. In addition, you use your Charisma modifier when setting the saving throw DC for a bard spell you cast and when making an attack roll with one.

items: Spell save DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Charisma modifier
Spell attack modifier = your proficiency bonus + your Charisma modifier

entries: Wisdom is your spellcasting ability for cleric spells. The power of your spells comes from your devotion to your deity. You use your Wisdom whenever a cleric spell refers to your spellcasting ability. In addition, you use your Wisdom modifier when setting the saving throw DC for a cleric spell you cast and when making an attack roll with one.

items: Spell save DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Wisdom modifier
Spell attack modifier = your proficiency bonus + your Wisdom modifier

entries: Wisdom is your spellcasting ability for druid spells, since your magic draws upon your devotion and attunement to nature. You use your Wisdom whenever a spell refers to your spellcasting ability. In addition, you use your Wisdom modifier when setting the saving throw DC for a druid spell you cast and when making an attack roll with one.

items: Spell save DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Wisdom modifier
Spell attack modifier = your proficiency bonus + your Wisdom modifier

entries: Charisma is your spellcasting ability for paladin spells, since their power derives from the strength of your convictions. You use your Charisma whenever a spell refers to your spellcasting ability. In addition, you use your Charisma modifier when setting the saving throw DC for a paladin spell you cast and when making an attack roll with one.

items: Spell save DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Charisma modifier
Spell attack modifier = your proficiency bonus + your Charisma modifier

entries: Wisdom is your spellcasting ability for ranger spells, since your magic draws on your attunement to nature. You use your Wisdom whenever a spell refers to your spellcasting ability. In addition, you use your Wisdom modifier when setting the saving throw DC for a ranger spell you cast and when making an attack roll with one.

items: Spell save DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Wisdom modifier
Spell attack modifier = your proficiency bonus + your Wisdom modifier

entries: Charisma is your spellcasting ability for sorcerer spells, since the power of your magic relies on your ability to project your will into the world. You use your Charisma whenever a spell refers to your spellcasting ability. In addition, you use your Charisma modifier when setting the saving throw DC for a sorcerer spell you cast and when making an attack roll with one.

items: Spell save DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Charisma modifier
Spell attack modifier = your proficiency bonus + your Charisma modifier

entries: Charisma is your spellcasting ability for warlock spells. You use your Charisma whenever a spell refers to your spellcasting ability. In addition, you use your Charisma modifier when setting the saving throw DC for a warlock spell you cast and when making an attack roll with one.

items: Spell save DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Charisma modifier
Spell attack modifier = your proficiency bonus + your Charisma modifier

entries: Intelligence is your spellcasting ability for wizard spells, since you learn your spells through dedicated study and memorization. You use your Intelligence whenever a spell refers to your spellcasting ability. In addition, you use your Intelligence modifier when setting the saving throw DC for a wizard spell you cast and when making an attack roll with one.

items: Spell save DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Intelligence modifier
Spell attack modifier = your proficiency bonus + your Intelligence modifier

entries: Wizards use Intelligence as their spellcasting ability, which helps determine the saving throw DC for their spells.

items: You can cast any bard spell you know as a ritual if that spell has the ritual tag.
You can cast a cleric spell as a ritual if that spell has the ritual tag and you have the spell prepared.
You can cast a druid spell as a ritual if that spell has the ritual tag and you have the spell prepared.
You can cast a wizard spell as a ritual if that spell has the ritual, and you have the spell in your spellbook. You don't need to have the spell prepared.

entries.name: Spellcasting Focus Options
entries: You can use a musical instrument (found in chapter 5) as a spellcasting focus for your bard spells.
You can use a holy symbol (found in chapter 5) as a spellcasting focus for your cleric spells.
You can use a druidic focus (found in chapter 5) as a spellcasting focus for your druid spells.
You can use a holy symbol (found in chapter 5) as a spellcasting focus for your paladin spells.
You can use an arcane focus (found in chapter 5) as a spellcasting focus for your sorcerer spells.
You can use an arcane focus (found in chapter 5) as a spellcasting focus for your warlock spells.
You can use an arcane focus (found in chapter 5) as a spellcasting focus for your wizard spells.

entries.name: Bardic Inspiration
entries: You can inspire others through stirring words or music. To do so, you use a {@action bonus action} on your turn to choose one creature other than yourself within {@range 60 feet} of you who can hear you. That creature gains one Bardic Inspiration die, a {@die d6}.

Once within the next 10 minutes, the creature can roll the die and add the number rolled to one ability check, attack roll, or saving throw it makes. The creature can wait until after it rolls the d20 before deciding to use the Bardic Inspiration die, but must decide before the DM says whether the roll succeeds or fails. Once the Bardic Inspiration die is rolled, it is {@condition lost}.
A creature can have only one Bardic Inspiration die at a time.
You can use this feature a number of times equal to your {@ability modifier Charisma} (a minimum of once). You regain any expended uses when you finish a {@action long rest}.
Your Bardic Inspiration die changes when you reach certain levels in this class. The die becomes a {@die d8} at 5th level, a {@die d10} at 10th level, and a {@die d12} at 15th level.

entries.name: Jack of All Trades
entries.description: Starting at 2nd level, you can add half your proficiency bonus, rounded down, to any ability check you make that doesn't already include your proficiency bonus.

entries.name: Song of Rest
entries: Beginning at 2nd level, you can use soothing music or oration to help revitalize your wounded allies during a short rest. If you or any friendly creatures who can hear your performance regain hit points at the end of the short rest, each of those creatures regains an extra {@damage 1d6} hit points.

entries.name: Bard College
entries: At 3rd level, you choose between the College of Lore or the College of Valor, both detailed at the end of the class description. Your choice grants features at 3rd level and again at 6th and 14th level.
At 3rd level, choose two of your skill proficiencies. Your proficiency bonus is doubled for any ability check you make that uses either of the chosen proficiencies.
At 10th level, you can choose another two skill proficiencies to gain this benefit.

At 1st level, choose two of your skill proficiencies, or one of your skill proficiencies and your proficiency with thieves' tools. Your proficiency bonus is doubled for any ability check you make that uses either of the chosen proficiencies.
At 6th level, you can choose two more of your proficiencies (in skills or with thieves' tools) to gain this benefit.

entries.text: Beginning when you reach 5th level, you regain all of your expended uses of Bardic Inspiration when you finish a short or long rest.

entries.name: Countercharm
entries.description: As an action, you can cast a performance that lasts until the end of your next turn. During that time, you and any friendly creatures within 30 feet of you have advantage on saving throws against being frightened or charmed. A creature must be able to hear you to gain this benefit. The performance ends early if you are incapacitated, silenced, or if you voluntarily end it (no action required).

entries: By 10th level, you have plundered magical knowledge from a wide spectrum of disciplines. Choose two spells from any class, including this one. A spell you choose must be a level you can cast, as shown on the Bard table, or a cantrip.
The chosen spells count as bard spells for you and are included in the number in the Spells Known column of the Bard table.
You learn two additional spells from any class at 14th level and again at 18th level.

entries.name: Superior Inspiration
entries.description: At 20th level, when you roll initiative and have no uses of Bardic Inspiration left, you regain one use.

entries.name: Bard Colleges
entries: Bards form loose associations called colleges to facilitate gatherings and preserve their traditions. These colleges serve as hubs for bards to swap songs and stories, boast of their accomplishments, and share knowledge.

entries.name: College of Lore
entries: Bards of the College of Lore dedicate themselves to the pursuit of beauty and truth, gathering knowledge from diverse sources such as scholarly tomes and peasant tales. They use their talents to captivate audiences through song, whether in taverns or royal courts, often leaving listeners questioning their beliefs and loyalties.
These bards prioritize honesty over political expediency, refusing to feign loyalty to monarchs or deities. A noble employing such a bard as an advisor can expect candid truths over flattery.

Members gather in libraries, academic institutions, and public events like festivals or state affairs. Here, they share lore, expose corruption, unravel lies, and mock self-important figures of authority.

entries.name: College of Lore
entries: At 3rd level, you gain proficiency with three skills of your choice.

entries.name: College of Valor
entries: At 3rd level, you gain proficiency with medium armor, shields, and martial weapons.

entries.name: General Proficiencies
entries: At 1st level, you gain proficiency with martial weapons and heavy armor.

entries.name: Archetype Features
entries: When you choose this archetype at 3rd level, you gain proficiency with the disguise kit and the poisoner's kit.

entries.name: Cutting Words
entries.description: At 3rd level, you can use your reaction to expend one use of Bardic Inspiration, rolling a Bardic Inspiration die and subtracting the number rolled from a creature's roll. This applies to attack rolls, ability checks, or damage rolls made by a creature you can see within 60 feet. You can choose to use this feature after the creature makes its roll, but before the DM determines whether the attack roll or ability check succeeds or fails, or before the creature deals its damage. The creature is immune if it can't hear you or if it's immune to being charmed.

entries.name: Additional Magical Secrets
entries.description: At 6th level, you learn two spells of your choice from any class. A spell you choose must be of a level you can cast, as shown on the Bard table, or a cantrip. The chosen spells count as bard spells for you but don't count against the number of bard spells you know.

entries.name: Peerless Skill
entries.description: When you make an ability check, you can expend one use of Bardic Inspiration. Roll a Bardic Inspiration die and add the number rolled to your ability check. You can choose to do so after you roll the die for the ability check, but before the DM tells you whether you succeed or fail.

entries.name: College of Valor
entries: Bards of the College of Valor are daring skalds whose tales keep alive the memory of the great heroes of the past, inspiring a new generation of heroes. These bards gather in mead halls or around great bonfires to sing the deeds of the mighty, both past and present. They travel the land to witness great events firsthand and ensure that the memory of those events does not pass from the world. With their songs, they inspire others to reach the same heights of accomplishment as the heroes of old.

entries.name: Combat Inspiration
entries.description: A creature that has a Bardic Inspiration die from you can roll that die and add the number rolled to a weapon damage roll it just made. Alternatively, when an attack roll is made against the creature, it can use its reaction to roll the Bardic Inspiration die and add the number rolled to its AC against that attack, after seeing the roll but before knowing whether it hits or misses.

entries.name: Battle Magic Feature
entries: At 14th level, you have mastered the art of weaving spellcasting and weapon use into a single harmonious act. When you use your action to cast a bard spell, you can make one weapon attack as a bonus action.

entries.name: Class Overview
entries: Clerics are conduits for divine magic, channeling the power of the gods to heal, inspire allies, and harm foes. They combine healing magic with combat prowess, using divine spells to provoke awe, lay curses, and call down divine retribution.

Clerics are rare and chosen by the gods, often taking up adventuring lives to fulfill their divine calling. They may protect their deities' worshipers, combat monsters, negotiate peace, or seal dangerous portals.

items: Spell slots per level increase with class level (see table for exact progression)

entries.name: Proficiency Bonus
entries: 1st level: +2
2nd level: +2
3rd level: +2
4th level: +2
5th level: +3
6th level: +3
7th level: +3
8th level: +3
9th level: +4
10th level: +4
11th level: +4
12th level: +4
13th level: +5
14th level: +5
15th level: +5
16th level: +5
17th level: +6
18th level: +6
19th level: +6
20th level: +6

items: Channel Divinity (3/day at 2nd level, 6/day at 6th level, 9/day at 10th level)
Destroy Undead (CR 1/2 at 5th level, CR 1 at 12th level, CR 2 at 11th level, CR 3 at 14th level, CR 4 at 17th level)
Divine Intervention (once per long rest at 10th level, improved at 20th level)

entries.name: Cantrips Known
entries: Starts with 3 cantrips, increasing by 1 at each level beyond 1st (maximum 5 cantrips at 17th level)

entries.name: Lore
entries: Divine magic is granted by the gods to chosen individuals, not through study but through devotion and an intuitive connection to a deity's will.
Not all temple servants are clerics; some serve through prayer and sacrifice without magical or combat prowess.
Clerics often maintain ties to their faith's temples or orders, receiving guidance or demands from high priests.

entries.name: Creating a Cleric
entries: When creating a cleric, the most important decision is selecting a deity and determining the principles your character embodies. Appendix B provides lists of multiversal gods; consult your DM to determine which deities are relevant to your campaign.

Consider your cleric's relationship with their chosen deity: Did they willingly enter service, or was the deity's choice imposed upon them? How do their temple priests view them—as a champion or a disruptor? Define ultimate goals: Does the deity have a specific task in mind, or is the character seeking to prove themselves worthy of a grand quest?

items: a shield and a holy symbol

entries.name: Spell Slots and Preparation
entries: The Cleric table shows how many spell slots you have to cast your spells of 1st level and higher. To cast one of these spells, you must expend a slot of the spell's level or higher. You regain all expended spell slots when you finish a long rest.

You prepare the list of cleric spells that are available for you to cast, choosing from the cleric spelllist. When you do so, choose a number of cleric spells equal to your Wisdom modifier + your cleric level (minimum of one spell). The spells must be of a level for which you have spell slots.

For example, if you are a 3rd-level cleric, you have four 1st-level and two 2nd-level spell slots. With a Wisdom of 16, your list of prepared spells can include six spells of 1st or 2nd level, in any combination. If you prepare the 1st-level spell *Cure Wounds*, you can cast it using a 1st-level or 2nd-level slot. Casting the spell doesn't remove it from your list of prepared spells.

You can change your list of prepared spells when you finish a long rest. Preparing a new list of cleric spells requires time spent in prayer and meditation: at least 1 minute per spell level for each spell on your list.

The Druid table shows how many spell slots you have to cast your spells of 1st level and higher. To cast one of these druid spells, you must expend a slot of the spell's level or higher. You regain all expended spell slots when you finish a long rest.

You prepare the list of druid spells that are available for you to cast, choosing from the druid spell list. When you do so, choose a number of druid spells equal to your Wisdom modifier + your druid level (minimum of one spell). The spells must be of a level for which you have spell slots.

The Paladin table shows how many spell slots you have to cast your spells. To cast one of your paladin spells of 1st level or higher, you must expend a slot of the spell's level or higher. You regain all expended spell slots when you finish a long rest.

You prepare the list of paladin spells that are available for you to cast, choosing from the paladin spell list. When you do so, choose a number of paladin spells equal to your Charisma modifier + half your paladin level, rounded down (minimum of one spell). The spells must be of a level for which you have spell slots.

For example, if you are a 5th-level paladin, you have four 1st-level and two 2nd-level spell slots. With a Charisma of 14, your list of prepared spells can include four spells of 1st or 2nd level, in any combination. If you prepare the 1st-level spell *Cure Wounds*, you can cast it using a 1st-level or a 2nd-level slot. Casting the spell doesn't remove it from your list of prepared spells.

You can change your list of prepared spells when you finish a long rest. Preparing a new list of paladin spells requires time spent in prayer and meditation: at least 1 minute per spell level for each spell on your list.

entries.name: Divine Domain Selection
entries: Choose one domain related to your deity: Knowledge, Life, Light, Nature, Tempest, Trickery, or War.
Each domain provides domain spells and features when chosen at 1st level.
Additional ways to use Channel Divinity are granted at 2nd level.
Additional benefits are granted at 6th, 8th, and 17th levels.
Each domain has a list of spells—its domain spells—that you gain at the cleric levels noted in the domain description.
Once you gain a domain spell, you always have it prepared, and it doesn't count against the number of spells you can prepare each day.
If you have a domain spell that doesn't appear on the cleric spelllist, the spell is nonetheless a cleric spell for you.

entries.name: Channel Divinity Feature
entries: At 2nd level, you gain the ability to channel divine energy directly from your deity, using that energy to fuel magical effects. You start with two such effects: Turn Undead and an effect determined by your domain. Some domains grant additional effects as you advance in levels, as noted in the domain description.
When you use your Channel Divinity, you choose which effect to create. You must then finish a short or long rest to use your Channel Divinity again.
Some Channel Divinity effects require saving throws. When you use such an effect from this class, the DC equals your cleric spell save DC.

Beginning at 6th level, you can use your Channel Divinity twice between rests, and beginning at 18th level, you can use it three times between rests. When you finish a short or long rest, you regain your expended uses.

entries.name: Sacred Weapon
entries: As an action, you can imbue one weapon you are holding with positive energy, using your Channel Divinity. For 1 minute, you add your Charisma modifier to attack rolls made with that weapon (minimum bonus of +1). The weapon emits bright light in a 20-foot radius and dim light 20 feet beyond that. If the weapon is not already magical, it becomes magical for the duration.
You can end this effect on your turn as part of any other action. If you are no longer holding or carrying this weapon, or if you fall unconscious, this effect ends.

entries.name: Turn the Unholy
entries: As an action, you present your holy symbol and speak a prayer censuring fiends and undead, using your Channel Divinity. Each fiend or undead that can see or hear you within 30 feet must make a Wisdom saving throw. If the creature fails, it is turned for 1 minute or until it takes damage.

A turned creature must spend its turns trying to move as far away from you as it can, and it can't willingly move to a space within 30 feet of you. It also can't take reactions. For its action, it can use only the Dash action or try to escape from an effect that prevents it from moving. If there's nowhere to move, the creature can use the Dodge action.
If the creature's true form is concealed by an illusion, shapeshifting, or other effect, that form is revealed while it is turned.

entries.name: Nature's Wrath
entries: As an action, you can cause spectral vines to spring up and reach for a creature within 10 feet of you that you can see. The creature must succeed on a Strength or Dexterity saving throw (its choice) or be restrained. While restrained by the vines, the creature repeats the saving throw at the end of each of its turns. On a success, it frees itself and the vines vanish.

entries.name: Turn the Faithless
entries: As an action, you present your holy symbol, and each fey or fiend within 30 feet of you that can hear you must make a Wisdom saving throw. On a failed save, the creature is turned for 1 minute or until it takes damage.

A turned creature must spend its turns trying to move as far away from you as it can, and it can't willingly move to a space within 30 feet of you. It also can't take reactions.
